title: How often should I bring my dog for a checkup?
body: Dogs should visit the vet at least once a year for a routine wellness exam. Puppies under one year need more frequent visits — typically at 8 weeks, 12 weeks, 16 weeks, and then at 6 months for spay/neuter consultation. Senior dogs aged 7 and older benefit from bi-annual checkups since age-related conditions can develop quickly. During a wellness exam the vet will check weight, teeth, heart, lungs, skin, and update vaccinations as needed.

title: What vaccinations does my puppy need?
body: Core vaccinations for puppies include: Distemper, Parvovirus, Adenovirus (given as a combo called DA2PP or DHPP) starting at 6–8 weeks and repeated every 3–4 weeks until 16 weeks old. Rabies vaccine is given at 12–16 weeks. Non-core vaccines like Bordetella (kennel cough), Leptospirosis, and Canine Influenza are recommended based on lifestyle and risk. Your vet will create a schedule tailored to your puppy.

title: What vaccinations does my cat need?
body: Core vaccinations for cats include: FVRCP (Feline Viral Rhinotracheitis, Calicivirus, Panleukopenia) starting at 6–8 weeks, boosted every 3–4 weeks until 16 weeks. Rabies is required by law in most states. Non-core vaccines include FeLV (Feline Leukemia Virus) — strongly recommended for cats that go outdoors. Indoor-only cats still need core vaccines. Adults need boosters every 1–3 years depending on the vaccine type.

title: How often should I bring my cat for a checkup?
body: Cats should have a wellness exam once a year. Kittens need visits at 8, 12, and 16 weeks for vaccinations and development checks. Senior cats aged 10 and older should be seen every 6 months. Cats are experts at hiding illness — by the time symptoms are obvious, disease may be advanced. Annual bloodwork is recommended for cats over 7 to catch kidney disease, thyroid problems, and diabetes early.

title: How do I know if my dog is a healthy weight?
body: Use the Body Condition Score (BCS) — a 9-point scale where 4–5 is ideal. You should be able to feel your dog's ribs easily without pressing hard, but not see them visibly. Looking from above, there should be a visible waist. From the side, the abdomen should tuck up behind the ribcage. Overweight dogs (BCS 6–9) have ribs that are hard to feel and no visible waist. Obesity in dogs increases risk of diabetes, joint disease, heart disease, and cancer.

title: How often should I deworm my dog?
body: Puppies should be dewormed at 2, 4, 6, and 8 weeks of age, then monthly until 6 months. Adult dogs should be dewormed at least 2–4 times per year depending on lifestyle. Dogs that hunt, eat wildlife, or spend time outdoors need more frequent treatment. Year-round heartworm prevention (monthly chewables or topicals) also covers some intestinal parasites. Ask your vet to run a fecal test annually to check for parasites not covered by standard dewormers.

title: At what age should I spay or neuter my dog?
body: The traditional recommendation is 6 months for small and medium breeds. For large and giant breeds (over 45 lbs), emerging research suggests waiting until 12–18 months allows hormones to support proper skeletal development and may reduce joint disease risk. The decision depends on breed, sex, and lifestyle. Spaying before the first heat cycle significantly reduces mammary tumor risk. Your vet will recommend the best timing for your specific dog.

title: How do I know if my cat is in pain?
body: Cats hide pain instinctively. Signs of pain include: hiding more than usual, reduced grooming or over-grooming one spot, changes in litter box habits, reduced appetite, growling or hissing when touched, reluctance to jump, squinting or partially closed eyes, flattened ears, and a hunched posture. Facial grimacing — whiskers pulled back, eyes narrowed, ears rotated — is a reliable pain indicator. If your cat is showing any of these signs, a vet visit is warranted.

title: What human foods are toxic to dogs?
body: Foods that are toxic to dogs include: grapes and raisins (can cause kidney failure), chocolate (theobromine toxicity), xylitol (found in sugar-free gum and some peanut butter — causes liver failure and low blood sugar), onions and garlic (damage red blood cells), macadamia nuts (neurological symptoms), alcohol, caffeine, avocado, and raw yeast dough. Even small amounts of xylitol or grapes can be life-threatening. If ingested, contact your vet or ASPCA Poison Control immediately.

title: What human foods are toxic to cats?
body: Foods toxic to cats include: onions and garlic (destroy red blood cells), raw fish (destroys vitamin B1 causing neurological problems), grapes and raisins (kidney failure), chocolate and caffeine, xylitol, alcohol, and raw eggs. Tuna in large amounts can cause mercury poisoning and nutritional deficiency. Milk and dairy cause digestive upset in most adult cats who are lactose intolerant. Bones from fish or poultry can splinter and cause internal injury.

title: How often should I brush my dog's teeth?
body: Daily brushing is ideal — dental disease is the most common health problem in dogs, affecting 80% of dogs over age 3. Use a dog-specific toothbrush and toothpaste (never human toothpaste — xylitol is toxic). If daily brushing isn't possible, 3–4 times per week makes a significant difference. Dental chews, water additives, and dental diets provide supplemental support but do not replace brushing. Professional dental cleanings under anesthesia are recommended annually or as your vet advises.

title: How long do dogs and cats live?
body: Small dogs (under 20 lbs) typically live 12–16 years. Medium dogs (20–50 lbs) live 10–14 years. Large dogs (50–90 lbs) live 8–12 years. Giant breeds (over 90 lbs) average 6–10 years. Cats typically live 12–18 years, with indoor cats often reaching 15–20 years. Mixed breed animals often live longer than purebreds due to greater genetic diversity. Proper nutrition, regular vet care, dental hygiene, and healthy weight significantly extend lifespan.

title: My dog is limping — what could it be?
body: Limping in dogs can range from minor to serious. EMERGENCY — seek immediate care if: the dog cannot bear any weight, the limb is visibly deformed or swollen, there is an open wound or bone visible, the dog cries constantly, or the dog was hit by a vehicle. URGENT — see vet within 24 hours if: limping has persisted more than 24 hours, swelling is present, or the dog is licking the leg obsessively. ROUTINE — schedule appointment if: mild limping that comes and goes, especially in older dogs (may indicate arthritis). Common causes include: thorn or cut in paw pad, sprained ligament, cruciate ligament tear (very common in dogs), hip dysplasia, arthritis, bone fracture, or Lyme disease.

title: My dog is vomiting — is it an emergency?
body: EMERGENCY — call emergency vet immediately if: vomiting blood or material that looks like coffee grounds, dry heaving without producing vomit (bloat — life-threatening in large breeds), bloated or distended abdomen with retching, vomiting after eating something toxic, dog is collapsed or unresponsive, or vomiting combined with extreme lethargy. URGENT — see vet within 12 hours if: vomiting more than 3 times in 24 hours, vomiting combined with diarrhea, dog cannot keep water down. ROUTINE — monitor at home if: one or two isolated vomiting episodes, dog is alert and active, vomiting stops within a few hours. Withhold food for 4–6 hours then offer small amounts of bland food (boiled chicken and rice).

title: My cat is vomiting — when should I be concerned?
body: Occasional vomiting in cats (1–2 times per month) is common and often related to hairballs or eating too fast. EMERGENCY — seek immediate care if: vomiting blood, vomiting combined with complete lethargy, inability to stand, vomiting after known toxin exposure, or abdomen appears swollen and painful. URGENT — see vet within 24 hours if: vomiting more than 3 times in one day, cat has not eaten in 24 hours, vomiting combined with weight loss, or yellow/green bile is present. ROUTINE — monitor if: single episode, cat is alert and interested in food, no other symptoms. Note: cats should never go more than 24–48 hours without eating — hepatic lipidosis (fatty liver disease) can develop rapidly.

title: My dog is not eating — what does it mean?
body: EMERGENCY — seek care immediately if: dog has not eaten for more than 48 hours AND is lethargic, vomiting, or has diarrhea. Also seek emergency care if the dog ate something potentially toxic or a foreign object. URGENT — see vet within 24 hours if: complete food refusal lasting more than 24 hours in a dog that normally has a good appetite, or loss of appetite combined with excessive thirst, weight loss, or pale gums. ROUTINE — monitor for 24 hours if: mild appetite reduction, no other symptoms, dog is alert and active. Common causes: dental pain (very common — check for bad breath), nausea, stress or anxiety, new food introduced too quickly, infection, kidney or liver disease.

title: My cat is not using the litter box — what does it mean?
body: Litter box avoidance in cats is one of the most common reasons for vet visits and always warrants investigation. EMERGENCY — seek immediate care if: male cat is straining to urinate and producing no urine (urinary blockage — life-threatening within hours), crying in pain when trying to urinate, or blood in urine combined with straining. URGENT — see vet within 24 hours if: cat is urinating frequently in small amounts, crying while urinating, licking genitals excessively, or avoiding the box completely. ROUTINE — schedule appointment if: occasional accidents outside the box, box aversion that developed gradually. Common causes: urinary tract infection, bladder stones, stress-related idiopathic cystitis, kidney disease, or behavioral issues.

title: My dog has diarrhea — when is it serious?
body: EMERGENCY — seek immediate care if: diarrhea contains large amounts of blood (red or black/tarry), dog is collapsed or extremely weak, diarrhea combined with vomiting and the dog cannot keep water down, or diarrhea in a puppy under 6 months that continues more than 12 hours. URGENT — see vet within 24 hours if: diarrhea has lasted more than 48 hours, dog is showing signs of dehydration (sunken eyes, dry gums, skin that does not spring back when pinched), or there is a small amount of blood in stool. ROUTINE — home management if: single episode of loose stool, dog is alert and drinking water. Withhold food for 12 hours, then feed small amounts of bland food. Ensure access to fresh water to prevent dehydration.

title: My dog is scratching excessively — what are the causes?
body: Excessive scratching in dogs is almost always due to one of four causes: allergies (environmental, food, or flea), skin infection (bacterial or yeast), parasites (fleas, mites, mange), or dry skin. EMERGENCY — not typically an emergency unless skin is severely broken, infected, and the dog has a fever. URGENT — see vet within 48 hours if: skin is raw, bleeding, or has oozing sores, hair loss is significant, or the dog is scratching to the point of self-injury. ROUTINE — schedule appointment if: chronic itching, seasonal patterns, or the dog has been on flea prevention and is still scratching. Common areas affected: ears (yeast), paws (allergies), base of tail (fleas), armpits and groin (environmental allergies).

title: My cat has stopped grooming — what does it mean?
body: Cats spend up to 50% of waking hours grooming. A sudden stop in grooming is always a sign that something is wrong. EMERGENCY — seek care immediately if: combined with complete lethargy, refusal to eat, or labored breathing. URGENT — see vet within 24 hours if: coat has become matted or greasy over days, cat appears stiff or painful when trying to reach parts of its body, or the cat appears generally unwell. ROUTINE — schedule appointment if: gradual coat decline over weeks. Common causes: arthritis (pain prevents reaching certain areas), obesity (cannot physically reach areas), dental pain, depression or stress, systemic illness such as kidney disease, hyperthyroidism, or diabetes.

title: Signs that always require emergency veterinary care
body: The following symptoms in any pet — dog, cat, or other — always require immediate emergency veterinary care and should not wait until the next morning: seizures or convulsions, collapse or sudden inability to stand, severe difficulty breathing or open-mouth breathing in cats, suspected poisoning or toxin ingestion, uncontrolled bleeding, bloated or distended abdomen especially with retching or dry heaving, eye injury or sudden loss of vision, suspected broken bones, urinary blockage especially in male cats, pale or white gums, extreme sudden lethargy or unresponsiveness, and trauma from a vehicle, fall from height, or animal attack. When in doubt, always call the emergency vet — it is always better to call and be told it can wait than to wait and have the condition become life-threatening.

title: My dog is drinking excessively — what does it mean?
body: Excessive drinking (polydipsia) in dogs is a clinically significant symptom that warrants a vet visit within 24–48 hours. It is rarely an emergency on its own but is often an early sign of serious underlying disease. Common causes include: diabetes mellitus (also shows excessive urination and weight loss despite good appetite), Cushing's disease (also shows pot belly, hair loss, increased appetite), kidney disease (also shows weight loss and poor appetite), pyometra in unspayed females (life-threatening uterine infection — also shows lethargy and vaginal discharge), liver disease, and hypercalcemia. Your vet will run bloodwork and urinalysis to identify the cause. Note the approximate amount your dog is drinking — this helps the vet assess severity.

title: What is Apoquel used for?
body: Apoquel (oclacitinib) is an oral medication used in dogs to control itching and inflammation associated with allergic skin disease, including atopic dermatitis (environmental allergies) and allergic dermatitis from flea or food allergies. It works by targeting specific enzymes (JAK inhibitors) involved in the itch-and-inflammation signal pathway rather than suppressing the entire immune system. It typically starts working within 4 hours and provides significant relief within 24 hours. Apoquel is approved for dogs over 12 months of age. It is a prescription medication — a valid vet-client-patient relationship and prescription are required. Common side effects are uncommon but may include vomiting, diarrhea, and lethargy. It should not be used in dogs with serious infections or cancer.

title: What is Cytopoint and how is it different from Apoquel?
body: Cytopoint (lokivetmab) is an injectable biologic medication for dogs that treats atopic dermatitis (environmental allergies) and associated itching. It works by targeting and neutralizing canine IL-31, a key protein that triggers the itch signal. A single injection provides relief for 4–8 weeks in most dogs. Unlike Apoquel, which is a daily oral medication, Cytopoint is administered at the vet clinic every 4–8 weeks. It is considered very safe because it acts like a natural antibody rather than a drug, and has minimal systemic side effects. Some dogs respond better to Apoquel, others to Cytopoint — vets sometimes use both together for severe cases. Both require a prescription and vet-client-patient relationship.

title: What is Heartgard and how does heartworm prevention work?
body: Heartgard (ivermectin + pyrantel) is a monthly chewable heartworm preventive for dogs. Heartworms are parasites transmitted by mosquitoes that live in the heart and pulmonary arteries. Heartgard does not kill adult heartworms — it kills the larval stage (microfilariae) that mosquitoes deposit during a bite, preventing them from developing into adults. It must be given every 30 days year-round for reliable protection. Before starting or restarting heartworm prevention, a heartworm blood test is required — giving preventives to a dog with adult heartworms can cause a life-threatening reaction. Heartgard also treats and controls roundworms and hookworms. It requires a prescription.

title: What is NexGard used for?
body: NexGard (afoxolaner) is a monthly oral chewable for dogs that kills fleas and ticks. It works by over-stimulating the nervous system of parasites through a mechanism that is highly selective for insects and arachnids and has minimal effect on mammals. It starts killing fleas within 4 hours and ticks within 8 hours of administration. It is effective against black-legged ticks (which carry Lyme disease), American dog ticks, brown dog ticks, and lone star ticks. NexGard is prescription-only, approved for dogs 8 weeks and older weighing over 4 lbs. It should not be used in dogs with a history of seizures without vet guidance. It does not prevent heartworm — a separate heartworm preventive is needed.

title: What is Carprofen (Rimadyl) used for in dogs?
body: Carprofen (brand name Rimadyl) is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) used in dogs to relieve pain and inflammation. It is commonly prescribed for osteoarthritis, post-surgical pain, and soft tissue pain. It works by inhibiting prostaglandins, the chemicals responsible for pain and inflammation. Carprofen should always be given with food to reduce stomach upset. It requires a prescription and regular monitoring — long-term use requires periodic bloodwork to check liver and kidney function. It should never be combined with other NSAIDs or steroids. Signs of adverse reaction include vomiting, diarrhea, black tarry stools, jaundice, or behavioral changes — stop medication and contact your vet immediately if these occur. Never give human NSAIDs (ibuprofen, aspirin, naproxen) to dogs — they are toxic.

title: What is Metronidazole used for in dogs and cats?
body: Metronidazole (brand names Flagyl, Metrobactin) is an antibiotic and antiprotozoal medication used in both dogs and cats to treat a range of gastrointestinal conditions. It is commonly prescribed for diarrhea caused by bacterial overgrowth, Giardia infection, Clostridium infection, and inflammatory bowel disease. It works by disrupting the DNA of anaerobic bacteria and parasites. It requires a prescription. Common side effects include drooling, nausea, and vomiting — giving with food usually helps. Neurological side effects (wobbling, disorientation) are rare but serious and require stopping the medication immediately. It should not be used in pregnant animals. The typical course is 5–10 days depending on the condition.

title: What is Gabapentin used for in pets?
body: Gabapentin is a medication used in dogs and cats for two main purposes: pain management and seizure control. For pain, it is commonly used for chronic neuropathic pain, arthritis, and post-surgical discomfort. It is often used alongside NSAIDs for multimodal pain management. For seizures, it is used as an add-on medication when primary anti-epileptic drugs do not fully control seizures. In cats it is also widely used as a pre-visit sedative to reduce anxiety and stress during vet appointments. Side effects include sedation and mild incoordination, especially when first starting. It requires a prescription. The dose varies significantly depending on the purpose — pain management doses are typically lower than seizure control doses.

title: What is Revolution (selamectin) used for?
body: Revolution (selamectin) is a topical monthly parasiticide applied to the skin at the back of the neck. In dogs, it prevents heartworm disease, kills adult fleas, prevents flea eggs from hatching, and treats ear mites and sarcoptic mange. In cats, it prevents heartworm, kills adult fleas, prevents flea eggs from hatching, treats ear mites, roundworms, and hookworms. It is absorbed through the skin into the bloodstream and distributed to skin, tissues, and GI tract. It is generally well tolerated — temporary hair loss at the application site is occasionally seen. It requires a prescription. Do not apply to broken skin or bathe the pet within 2 hours of application.

title: What is Prednisone used for in dogs and cats?
body: Prednisone and prednisolone are corticosteroids (steroids) used in dogs and cats to reduce inflammation and suppress the immune system. They are prescribed for a wide range of conditions including severe allergic reactions, immune-mediated diseases (immune-mediated hemolytic anemia, immune-mediated thrombocytopenia), inflammatory bowel disease, asthma in cats, certain cancers, Addison's disease, and severe skin conditions. They are powerful medications with significant side effects on long-term use including increased thirst and urination, increased appetite, weight gain, muscle wasting, and increased susceptibility to infection. They should never be stopped abruptly — dose must be tapered under vet guidance. They should not be combined with NSAIDs. Requires a prescription.

title: What is Onsior used for in cats?
body: Onsior (robenacoxib) is a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) specifically approved for cats to control pain and inflammation after soft tissue surgery. It is available as tablets or injection. It is one of very few NSAIDs approved for use in cats — most human and dog NSAIDs are toxic to cats due to their different metabolism. The tablet form is typically given for up to 3 days post-surgery. Like all NSAIDs, it should not be combined with steroids or other NSAIDs. It should not be used in cats with kidney disease, liver disease, or gastrointestinal problems. Requires a prescription. Never give dogs' NSAIDs to cats — even a single dose of carprofen or meloxicam at the wrong dose can cause acute kidney failure in cats.

title: ¿Con qué frecuencia debo llevar a mi perro al veterinario?
body: Los perros deben visitar al veterinario al menos una vez al año para un examen de bienestar de rutina. Los cachorros menores de un año necesitan visitas más frecuentes: generalmente a las 8 semanas, 12 semanas, 16 semanas y luego a los 6 meses. Los perros mayores de 7 años se benefician de chequeos cada 6 meses, ya que las condiciones relacionadas con la edad pueden desarrollarse rápidamente. Durante el examen, el veterinario revisará el peso, los dientes, el corazón, los pulmones, la piel y actualizará las vacunas según sea necesario.

title: ¿Qué alimentos son tóxicos para los perros?
body: Los alimentos tóxicos para perros incluyen: uvas y pasas (pueden causar insuficiencia renal), chocolate (toxicidad por teobromina), xilitol (presente en chicles sin azúcar y algunas mantequillas de maní — causa insuficiencia hepática y bajada de azúcar), cebollas y ajos (dañan los glóbulos rojos), nueces de macadamia, alcohol, cafeína, aguacate y masa de levadura cruda. Incluso pequeñas cantidades de xilitol o uvas pueden ser mortales. Si su perro ingirió alguno de estos alimentos, comuníquese con su veterinario o con el Control de Envenenamiento de la ASPCA de inmediato.

title: Mi perro está cojeando — ¿qué podría ser?
body: La cojera en perros puede variar de leve a grave. EMERGENCIA — busque atención inmediata si: el perro no puede apoyar ningún peso, la extremidad está visiblemente deformada o hinchada, hay una herida abierta o hueso visible, el perro llora constantemente, o fue atropellado por un vehículo. URGENTE — consulte al veterinario en 24 horas si: la cojera persiste más de 24 horas, hay hinchazón, o el perro lame obsesivamente la pata. RUTINA — programe una cita si: cojera leve que va y viene, especialmente en perros mayores (puede indicar artritis). Causas comunes: espina o corte en la almohadilla, esguince, desgarro del ligamento cruzado, displasia de cadera, artritis o fractura ósea.

title: Mi perro está vomitando — ¿es una emergencia?
body: EMERGENCIA — llame al veterinario de emergencias de inmediato si: vomita sangre o material parecido a posos de café, hace arcadas sin producir vómito (dilatación gástrica — mortal en razas grandes), tiene el abdomen distendido con arcadas, vomitó algo tóxico, está colapsado o no responde. URGENTE — consulte en 12 horas si: vomita más de 3 veces en 24 horas, el vómito se combina con diarrea, o no puede retener agua. RUTINA — vigile en casa si: uno o dos episodios aislados, el perro está alerta y activo. Retire el alimento durante 4–6 horas y luego ofrezca pequeñas cantidades de comida blanda (pollo hervido con arroz).

title: ¿Para qué se usa el Apoquel?
body: Apoquel (oclacitinib) es un medicamento oral que se usa en perros para controlar el picor y la inflamación asociados con enfermedades alérgicas de la piel, como la dermatitis atópica y la dermatitis alérgica por pulgas o alimentos. Actúa dirigiéndose a enzimas específicas (inhibidores JAK) involucradas en la señal de picor e inflamación. Generalmente comienza a actuar en 4 horas y brinda un alivio significativo en 24 horas. Está aprobado para perros mayores de 12 meses. Es un medicamento con receta — se requiere una relación válida veterinario-cliente-paciente. Los efectos secundarios son poco comunes pero pueden incluir vómitos, diarrea y letargo.

title: ¿Para qué se usa el Heartgard?
body: Heartgard (ivermectina + pirantel) es un preventivo masticable mensual contra el gusano del corazón en perros. Los gusanos del corazón son parásitos transmitidos por mosquitos que viven en el corazón y las arterias pulmonares. Heartgard no mata los gusanos adultos — mata la etapa larval que los mosquitos depositan durante la picadura. Debe administrarse cada 30 días durante todo el año. Antes de comenzar el preventivo, se requiere una prueba de sangre para detectar gusanos del corazón. Heartgard también trata y controla los áscaris y los anquilostomas. Requiere receta médica.

title: Immediate emergency signs — act now, do not wait
body: The following signs require calling an emergency vet immediately — do not wait until morning or the next available appointment: any seizure lasting more than 2 minutes or multiple seizures in 24 hours, collapse or sudden inability to stand or walk, open-mouth breathing or gasping in cats (always an emergency), severe difficulty breathing in any pet, suspected ingestion of poison, toxin, medication, or foreign object, uncontrolled bleeding that does not stop with 5 minutes of firm pressure, suspected broken bone with visible deformity, bloated or distended abdomen especially with unproductive retching in dogs, complete inability to urinate especially in male cats, pale, white, blue, or grey gums, eye trauma or sudden blindness, and body temperature below 99°F or above 104°F. When in doubt, always call — a 2-minute phone call to an emergency vet can save your pet's life.

title: What to do if your pet ingests something toxic
body: If your pet has ingested or you suspect ingestion of a toxic substance: do not wait for symptoms to appear — some toxins cause irreversible damage before symptoms show. Immediately call your veterinarian or the ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center at 888-426-4435 (a consultation fee may apply). Have the following information ready: the pet's species, breed, age, and weight, the name of the substance ingested, the estimated amount ingested, and when ingestion occurred. Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by a vet — this can cause additional harm with some substances such as caustics, petroleum products, or sharp objects. Bring the packaging or a sample of the substance to the vet if possible. Time is critical — the faster treatment begins, the better the outcome.

title: CPR basics for pets — when and how
body: Pet CPR should only be performed if the animal is unconscious and not breathing. Check for breathing: watch for chest rise, listen for breath sounds, and feel for air movement from the nose. Check for a heartbeat by placing your hand on the left side of the chest just behind the front leg. If there is no heartbeat and no breathing: for dogs, place both hands on the widest part of the chest, compress 30 times at 100 compressions per minute (1/3 to 1/2 of chest width), then give 2 rescue breaths by closing the mouth and breathing into the nose. For cats and small dogs, use one hand with thumb and fingers on either side of the chest. Continue until the pet breathes on its own or you reach emergency care. CPR is a bridge to emergency treatment — get to a vet immediately.

title: How much should I feed my dog?
body: Feeding amount depends on the dog's age, weight, activity level, and the specific food's caloric density. A general starting point is to follow the feeding guide on the food packaging, then adjust based on body condition. Most adult dogs do well with two meals per day — morning and evening. Free feeding (leaving food out all day) is not recommended as it makes it harder to monitor intake and contributes to obesity. Measure food by weight rather than volume for accuracy. Treats should not exceed 10% of daily caloric intake. Puppies need 3–4 meals per day until 6 months. Senior dogs may need fewer calories as metabolism slows. Always transition to new food gradually over 7–10 days to avoid digestive upset.

title: How much should I feed my cat?
body: Most adult cats need between 200–300 calories per day depending on size and activity level. Indoor cats tend to be less active and need fewer calories than outdoor cats. Cats are obligate carnivores and need a diet high in animal protein. Wet food is generally preferred over dry food as it provides hydration — cats have a low thirst drive and chronic mild dehydration contributes to kidney disease. If feeding dry food, ensure fresh water is always available. Puzzle feeders and timed feeders help prevent overeating and provide mental stimulation. Avoid sudden food changes — transition over 10–14 days. Kittens need kitten-specific food until 12 months due to higher protein and calorie requirements.

title: Is it safe to give my dog bones?
body: Raw meaty bones are generally safer than cooked bones. Cooked bones — especially chicken, turkey, and pork — become brittle when cooked and can splinter into sharp fragments that puncture the esophagus, stomach, or intestines. This is a medical emergency requiring surgery. Even raw bones carry risks including tooth fractures (especially hard weight-bearing bones like femurs), bacterial contamination (Salmonella, E. coli), and intestinal obstruction in dogs that swallow large chunks. If you choose to give bones, always supervise, choose raw bones appropriate for the dog's size, and remove the bone once it becomes small enough to swallow whole. Dental chews certified by the Veterinary Oral Health Council (VOHC) are a safer alternative for dental health.

title: When do puppies open their eyes?
body: Puppies are born with their eyes closed. They begin to open between 10–14 days of age. Vision is initially blurry and continues developing for several weeks. Ears also open around the same time, typically at 2–3 weeks. During this period puppies are entirely dependent on their mother for warmth, nutrition, and stimulation. The critical socialization window for puppies is 3–16 weeks — positive exposure to people, other animals, sounds, surfaces, and environments during this period shapes temperament for life. Puppies should not leave their mother and littermates before 8 weeks as early separation leads to behavioral problems.

title: How do I introduce a new dog to my existing dog?
body: Proper introduction reduces the risk of aggression and helps both dogs accept each other. Start with a neutral location — not your home where the existing dog is territorial. Walk both dogs parallel on leash with enough distance that they can see each other without reacting. Gradually close the distance over several minutes. Allow brief sniffing then separate and reward calm behavior. At home, keep them separated initially with a baby gate so they can see and smell each other safely. Feed separately to avoid resource guarding. Supervise all interactions for the first several weeks. Give the existing dog extra attention to avoid jealousy. Most dogs take 2–4 weeks to settle into a comfortable coexistence. Seek help from a certified behaviorist if serious aggression occurs.

title: What is kennel cough and how is it spread?
body: Kennel cough (infectious tracheobronchitis) is a highly contagious respiratory infection in dogs caused by a combination of bacteria (most commonly Bordetella bronchiseptica) and viruses (parainfluenza, adenovirus). The hallmark symptom is a harsh, honking cough that sounds like the dog has something stuck in its throat, sometimes followed by retching or gagging. It spreads through direct contact, airborne droplets, and contaminated surfaces — anywhere dogs gather including boarding facilities, dog parks, groomers, and training classes. Most cases resolve in 1–3 weeks. Puppies, elderly dogs, and immunocompromised dogs can develop severe pneumonia. The Bordetella vaccine is recommended for dogs with regular social exposure. See a vet if the dog has a fever, stops eating, or the cough persists more than 3 weeks.

title: What is parvovirus and how dangerous is it?
body: Canine parvovirus (CPV) is a highly contagious and potentially fatal viral disease that primarily affects puppies and unvaccinated dogs. It attacks the lining of the small intestine, causing severe bloody diarrhea, vomiting, lethargy, and loss of appetite. It can also attack bone marrow and the heart in very young puppies. The virus is transmitted through direct contact with infected feces and contaminated surfaces — it can survive on surfaces for months to years. Treatment is supportive (IV fluids, anti-nausea medication, antibiotics to prevent secondary infection) and survival rates with aggressive treatment are 68–92%. Without treatment survival drops to less than 10%. Prevention is through vaccination — the parvovirus vaccine is a core vaccine for all dogs. Puppies are most vulnerable before completing their vaccine series.

title: Can my dog get fleas even if they don't go outside much?
body: Yes. Fleas can enter your home on clothing, shoes, other pets, and through gaps in doors and windows. Fleas are incredibly resilient — a single female flea can lay up to 50 eggs per day. Only about 5% of a flea infestation lives on the pet — 95% of eggs, larvae, and pupae live in the environment (carpets, furniture, bedding). This is why treating only the pet is insufficient. Year-round flea prevention is recommended even for primarily indoor dogs. If an infestation occurs, treat all pets in the household simultaneously, wash all bedding in hot water, vacuum thoroughly and frequently, and use an environmental flea spray. It can take 3–4 months to fully eliminate an established infestation due to the resilient pupal stage.

title: What is hip dysplasia in dogs?
body: Hip dysplasia is a developmental condition where the hip joint does not form properly — the ball and socket do not fit snugly, causing abnormal wear, inflammation, and progressive arthritis. It is hereditary but environmental factors like rapid growth, high-calorie diet, and excessive exercise in puppyhood can worsen it. Large and giant breeds are most commonly affected — German Shepherds, Labrador Retrievers, Golden Retrievers, Rottweilers, and Great Danes are particularly prone. Symptoms include difficulty rising, reduced activity, bunny-hopping gait, lameness in the rear legs, and reluctance to climb stairs or jump. Diagnosis is by X-ray. Management includes weight control, controlled exercise, joint supplements, pain medication, and in severe cases, surgery (total hip replacement or femoral head ostectomy). Early detection through screening improves long-term outcomes.

title: How do I know if my dog has dental disease?
body: Dental disease (periodontal disease) affects 80% of dogs by age 3 and is the most common health problem seen in veterinary practice. Signs include: bad breath (most common early sign), yellow or brown tartar buildup on teeth, red or swollen gums, receding gums, loose or missing teeth, drooling, pawing at the mouth, dropping food, and reluctance to eat hard food or chew toys. Dental disease is often painful but dogs hide pain well — many owners are surprised to learn their dog has severe dental disease because the dog continued eating normally. Untreated dental disease allows bacteria to enter the bloodstream and can damage the heart, kidneys, and liver. Annual professional dental cleaning under anesthesia and home brushing are the most effective preventive measures.

title: What is the difference between a dog allergy and a dog cold?
body: Dogs do not get colds from the same viruses that cause human colds, though they can get their own respiratory infections. Kennel cough is the most common dog respiratory infection and causes coughing, not sneezing. Allergies in dogs primarily manifest as skin issues (itching, redness, hot spots, ear infections) rather than respiratory symptoms like in humans. However, some dogs do sneeze from environmental irritants, mold, or dust. Nasal discharge in dogs is worth investigating — clear discharge from allergies or mild infection, yellow or green discharge suggests bacterial infection, and bloody or dark discharge warrants urgent evaluation. Reverse sneezing (a snorting, honking sound) is common in dogs and usually harmless. If your dog has persistent nasal discharge, coughing, or sneezing lasting more than a few days, a vet visit is warranted.

title: What is bloat in dogs and why is it dangerous?
body: Gastric Dilatation-Volvulus (GDV), commonly called bloat, is a life-threatening emergency that can kill a dog within hours. The stomach fills with gas and twists on its axis, trapping gas inside and cutting off blood supply to the stomach and spleen. Without emergency surgery, the dog will die. Signs include: distended or bloated abdomen, unproductive retching or dry heaving, excessive drooling, restlessness and inability to get comfortable, rapid shallow breathing, pale gums, and collapse. Large, deep-chested breeds are most at risk: Great Danes, German Shepherds, Standard Poodles, Weimaraners, and Boxers. Risk factors include eating one large meal per day, eating rapidly, exercising immediately after meals, and stress. If you suspect bloat, go to an emergency vet immediately — do not wait.

title: How do I safely remove a tick from my dog?
body: Use fine-tipped tweezers or a tick removal tool. Grasp the tick as close to the skin surface as possible. Pull upward with steady, even pressure — do not twist or jerk, as this can cause the mouthparts to break off and remain in the skin. Do not squeeze the tick's body. After removal, clean the bite area with rubbing alcohol or soap and water. Dispose of the tick by placing it in alcohol, sealing it in a bag, or flushing it. Do not crush the tick with your fingers. Do not use petroleum jelly, nail polish, heat, or other home remedies to remove ticks — these methods are ineffective and may cause the tick to release more disease-causing agents. Monitor the bite site for signs of infection or rash. See a vet if the dog develops fever, lethargy, swollen joints, or loss of appetite within 2–4 weeks of tick exposure.

title: What is Lyme disease in dogs and what are the symptoms?
body: Lyme disease is caused by the bacterium Borrelia burgdorferi transmitted through the bite of infected black-legged ticks (deer ticks). It is most common in the Northeast, Upper Midwest, and Pacific Coast of the United States. Most infected dogs never show clinical signs. When symptoms do occur they typically appear 2–5 months after the tick bite and include: lameness that shifts from leg to leg, joint swelling, fever, loss of appetite, and lethargy. In a small percentage of dogs it causes serious kidney disease (Lyme nephritis) that can be fatal. Diagnosis is via blood test. Treatment is antibiotics, typically doxycycline for 28–30 days. A Lyme vaccine is available for dogs in endemic areas. Year-round tick prevention is the most effective protection.

title: Is it normal for my dog to eat grass?
body: Grass eating is extremely common in dogs and is generally not a concern. Studies show that the majority of grass-eating episodes are not preceded by illness and do not cause vomiting. Dogs may eat grass out of boredom, as a behavioral trait inherited from wild ancestors, because they enjoy the taste and texture, or to supplement dietary fiber. However, if a dog suddenly starts eating large amounts of grass and vomiting repeatedly, this can indicate gastrointestinal upset and warrants a vet visit. Occasional grass eating with occasional vomiting is normal. Ensure the grass your dog eats has not been treated with pesticides, herbicides, or fertilizers as these can be toxic. If grass eating is excessive and compulsive, it may indicate a nutritional deficiency or behavioral issue worth discussing with your vet.

title: Why does my cat knock things off tables?
body: Cats knock things off surfaces for several well-documented reasons rooted in their natural behavior. Predatory instinct: batting at objects mimics hunting behavior — the movement engages their prey drive. Attention-seeking: if knocking things off results in a reaction from their owner, cats quickly learn it is an effective way to get attention. Curiosity and environmental testing: cats use their paws to explore the physical properties of objects. Boredom or understimulation: cats with insufficient play and enrichment find their own entertainment. The behavior is normal and not a sign of behavioral problems. Reducing it requires: providing adequate play and enrichment, ignoring the behavior to avoid reinforcing it with attention, and securing valuable objects. Puzzle feeders, window perches, and interactive toys reduce boredom-driven behaviors.

title: Why does my cat bring me dead animals?
body: Cats that hunt and bring prey to their owners are displaying a natural nurturing behavior. In the wild, mother cats bring prey to their kittens to teach them to hunt. Domestic cats that bring prey to their owners are exhibiting this same instinct — they are treating their owner as a family member they want to provide for or teach. This behavior is more common in female cats and cats with strong prey drives. It is a compliment, not a behavioral problem. To reduce hunting: keep cats indoors or in a catio, attach a bell to the collar to warn prey, and ensure ample play with feather wands and interactive toys to redirect predatory energy. Punishing a cat for bringing prey is ineffective and confusing to the cat.

title: What is feline lower urinary tract disease (FLUTD)?
body: Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD) is an umbrella term for several conditions affecting the bladder and urethra in cats. It is very common, especially in middle-aged, overweight, indoor cats fed primarily dry food. Symptoms include: straining to urinate, frequent trips to the litter box with little or no output, blood in urine, urinating outside the litter box, and excessive licking of the genitals. In male cats, FLUTD can cause a complete urinary blockage which is life-threatening within 24–48 hours. Causes include: idiopathic cystitis (stress-related, most common), urinary stones, infection, and anatomical abnormalities. Treatment depends on the cause. Prevention includes: wet food diet to increase water intake, stress reduction, multiple litter boxes, and environmental enrichment.

title: Should I keep my cat indoors or let them go outside?
body: Indoor cats live significantly longer than outdoor cats — on average 12–18 years versus 2–5 years for outdoor cats. Outdoor cats face risks including: vehicle accidents, predation by coyotes and dogs, infectious diseases (FIV, FeLV, rabies), parasites, toxin exposure, and injury from other cats. However, indoor-only cats can suffer from boredom and obesity without adequate enrichment. The best of both worlds is a catio (enclosed outdoor space), leash training, or supervised outdoor time. For indoor-only cats, enrichment is essential: window perches, bird feeders outside windows, puzzle feeders, cat trees, interactive toys, and play sessions of at least 20–30 minutes daily. Indoor cats also require regular veterinary care — they still need vaccines, parasite prevention, and annual health checks.

title: What is hyperthyroidism in cats and what are the signs?
body: Hyperthyroidism is the most common hormonal disease in cats, typically affecting cats over 10 years old. It is caused by a benign overgrowth of thyroid tissue producing excess thyroid hormone, which speeds up metabolism. Signs include: weight loss despite increased or ravenous appetite, increased thirst and urination, hyperactivity or restlessness, a poor or greasy coat, vomiting, diarrhea, rapid heart rate, and an enlarged thyroid gland that may be felt in the neck. It is diagnosed with a blood test measuring T4 levels. Left untreated it causes heart disease, high blood pressure, and kidney damage. Treatment options include: daily oral medication (methimazole), radioactive iodine therapy (curative in most cases), surgical removal of the thyroid, or a prescription low-iodine diet. Most cats respond well to treatment.

title: What is kidney disease in cats and how common is it?
body: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) is the leading cause of death in cats, affecting an estimated 30–40% of cats over 10 years old. The kidneys lose function gradually over months to years. Because cats have significant kidney reserve, signs often don't appear until 75% of function is lost. Early signs include: increased thirst and urination, weight loss, reduced appetite, and occasional vomiting. Later signs include: severe weight loss, lethargy, poor coat, bad breath with an ammonia-like smell, mouth ulcers, and dehydration. Diagnosis is through blood and urine tests. There is no cure but progression can be slowed with: prescription kidney diet (low protein, low phosphorus), phosphate binders, fluids (subcutaneous fluids at home in advanced cases), anti-nausea medication, and blood pressure control. Annual bloodwork from age 7 is the best early detection tool.

title: Why does my cat knead with their paws?
body: Kneading — pushing paws alternately against a soft surface — is a comfort behavior that originates in kittenhood. Kittens knead their mother's mammary glands to stimulate milk flow. Adult cats retain this behavior and perform it when they feel safe, content, or relaxed. It is a sign of happiness and trust. Cats often knead on their favorite humans, soft blankets, or cat beds. Some cats knead before sleeping — similar to how dogs circle before lying down, it may originate from an instinct to pat down sleeping areas. If a cat's kneading becomes excessive or compulsive it may indicate anxiety, in which case environmental enrichment and stress reduction are helpful. Trimming nails regularly prevents kneading from being uncomfortable for human recipients.

title: What is feline leukemia virus (FeLV) and how is it spread?
body: Feline Leukemia Virus (FeLV) is a retrovirus that compromises the immune system of cats, making them susceptible to secondary infections and certain cancers. It is the second most common cause of death in cats after trauma. FeLV is spread through prolonged close contact — mutual grooming, shared food and water bowls, and biting. It is not easily spread by casual contact. Cats that go outdoors, live with FeLV-positive cats, or were exposed as kittens are at highest risk. Signs of FeLV infection include: weight loss, poor coat, pale gums, persistent infections, enlarged lymph nodes, and lethargy. There is no cure — management focuses on supportive care and treating secondary infections. The FeLV vaccine is highly effective and recommended for all at-risk cats. All new cats should be tested before being introduced to other cats.

title: What is the best litter for cats?
body: Cat litter preference varies between individual cats, which can make changing litters challenging. Most cats prefer fine-grained, unscented, clumping clay litter — it most closely mimics the texture of sand that cats naturally prefer for elimination. Avoid heavily scented litters — cats have a sense of smell 14 times more sensitive than humans, and artificial fragrances can deter litter box use. The litter box itself should be 1.5 times the length of the cat, uncovered (covered boxes trap odors), and scooped at least once daily. The general rule is one litter box per cat plus one extra. Place boxes in quiet, accessible locations — not near food or in high-traffic areas. If a cat is avoiding the litter box, the first step before assuming a behavioral problem is a vet check to rule out urinary or gastrointestinal issues.

title: My dog has a swollen face or muzzle — what does it mean?
body: Facial swelling in dogs most commonly indicates one of three things: allergic reaction, dental abscess, or insect sting. EMERGENCY — seek immediate care if: swelling developed rapidly (within minutes to an hour), the dog is having difficulty breathing, has hives over the body, is vomiting, or is collapsing — these signs indicate anaphylaxis, a life-threatening allergic reaction. Also seek emergency care if swelling is around the eye. URGENT — see vet within a few hours if: swelling is confined to one cheek or below the eye (suggests tooth root abscess), or there is discharge from a swelling. ROUTINE — monitor if: mild swelling after being outside that is not progressing and the dog is otherwise normal. Insect stings typically cause localized swelling that resolves within a few hours. Dental abscesses require antibiotics and often dental extraction.

title: My dog is shaking or trembling — what are the causes?
body: Trembling in dogs has many causes ranging from completely benign to life-threatening. EMERGENCY — seek immediate care if: trembling is accompanied by seizure-like activity (paddling, jaw chomping, loss of consciousness), the dog ate something potentially toxic in the past 4 hours, trembling began suddenly and the dog cannot stand, or the dog is also vomiting and has a distended abdomen. URGENT — see vet within 24 hours if: trembling persists for more than an hour without obvious cause, or is accompanied by other symptoms such as diarrhea, lethargy, or loss of appetite. BENIGN causes include: cold (small dogs and puppies shiver easily), excitement or anxiety, pain (may tremble without vocalizing), old dog vestibular disease. Common medical causes: hypoglycemia (low blood sugar, especially in toy breeds and puppies), toxin ingestion, kidney disease, Addison's disease, and neurological conditions.

title: My cat is breathing heavily or open-mouth breathing — is this an emergency?
body: Yes — open-mouth breathing in a cat is always an emergency. Unlike dogs, cats do not pant normally. Open-mouth breathing in cats indicates severe respiratory distress and requires immediate emergency veterinary care. Do not wait. While transporting the cat, keep the environment calm, minimize handling, and ensure good air flow. Common causes of respiratory distress in cats: congestive heart failure (fluid around the lungs), pleural effusion (fluid in chest cavity), asthma, pneumonia, heartworm disease, and trauma to the chest. Rapid shallow breathing, neck extended forward and elbows pointed outward, blue or grey gums, and obvious labored breathing are all signs of severe oxygen deprivation. Even if the cat seems to stabilize, respiratory distress is unstable and can deteriorate rapidly. Always go to an emergency vet.

title: My dog has red, itchy ears — what could it be?
body: Ear problems are among the most common reasons dogs visit the vet. Signs of ear infection: head shaking, scratching at ears, dark discharge, odor from ears, redness or swelling of the ear canal, tilting head to one side, and pain when ears are touched. EMERGENCY — seek immediate care if: dog is in severe pain, has a head tilt combined with falling or circling (suggests inner ear involvement or neurological issue), or ear is bleeding heavily. URGENT — see vet within 48 hours for any suspected ear infection — they worsen quickly and become more painful and expensive to treat if delayed. Common causes: yeast overgrowth (most common — brown discharge, musty smell), bacterial infection (yellow-green discharge, foul smell), ear mites (dark crumbly debris, common in cats and young dogs), foreign body (grass seed), or allergies. Never insert Q-tips into a dog's ear canal.

title: My dog is scooting on the floor — what does this mean?
body: Scooting — dragging the rear end along the floor — almost always indicates discomfort in the anal area. The most common cause is full or impacted anal glands. Anal glands are small scent glands located at 4 and 8 o'clock positions around the anus. In most dogs they express naturally when defecating, but some dogs require manual expression by a vet or groomer every 4–8 weeks. Other causes include: intestinal parasites (especially tapeworms — look for rice-like segments around the rear or in feces), skin irritation or rash around the anal area, rectal prolapse, or perineal fistulas in German Shepherds. URGENT — see vet within 48 hours if: scooting is combined with swelling, redness, or a draining tract near the anus (suggests anal gland abscess which is painful and requires lancing and antibiotics). Routine scooting should be evaluated at the next available appointment.

title: My dog has a lump or bump — should I be worried?
body: Not all lumps are cancerous, but all new lumps should be evaluated by a vet. EMERGENCY — seek immediate care if: a lump grew to a noticeable size in days, is bleeding significantly, or is accompanied by rapid weight loss, lethargy, or difficulty breathing. URGENT — see vet within a week if: the lump is growing, is ulcerated (has an open sore), is very firm and fixed to underlying tissue, or is in a concerning location such as the mammary chain, near lymph nodes, or on the face. ROUTINE — at next available appointment for: soft, moveable lumps that have been stable in size, especially in older dogs (likely lipomas — benign fatty tumors). The only way to definitively characterize a lump is cytology (needle aspirate) or biopsy. Common benign lumps: lipomas, sebaceous cysts, warts (papillomas), histiocytomas in young dogs. Common malignant tumors: mast cell tumors, melanoma, soft tissue sarcomas.

title: My dog or cat has blood in their urine — what does it mean?
body: Blood in urine (hematuria) is always worth a vet visit, though the urgency depends on accompanying symptoms. EMERGENCY — seek immediate care if: male cat is straining to urinate with no urine output (urinary blockage), dog or cat is in obvious pain, collapsed, or not responding normally. URGENT — see vet within 24 hours for any visible blood in urine combined with straining, frequent urination, or crying when urinating. ROUTINE — schedule appointment within a few days if: blood is found incidentally or appears just once with no other symptoms. Common causes in dogs: urinary tract infection (most common in females), bladder stones, bladder polyps or tumors, trauma, clotting disorders, and prostate problems in intact males. Common causes in cats: idiopathic cystitis (stress-related), urinary stones, infection (less common than in dogs), and bladder tumors. Bring a fresh urine sample to the appointment if possible.

title: My dog is limping after exercise — could it be arthritis?
body: Limping or stiffness that appears after exercise and improves with rest is a classic presentation of osteoarthritis in dogs. It is very common in middle-aged and older dogs, especially large breeds, overweight dogs, and dogs with a history of joint injuries. Other signs of arthritis: difficulty rising from rest, reluctance to climb stairs or jump into the car, decreased activity level, licking or chewing at joints, behavioral changes (grumpiness or withdrawal), and muscle wasting over the hindquarters. Arthritis is a progressive condition with no cure, but quality of life can be significantly improved with: weight management (most impactful single intervention), controlled low-impact exercise (swimming, leash walks), joint supplements (omega-3 fatty acids, glucosamine/chondroitin), pain medication (NSAIDs prescribed by vet), physical therapy, and acupuncture. Schedule a vet appointment for formal assessment and a pain management plan.

title: My cat has a wound or abscess — what should I do?
body: Cat bite abscesses are very common in cats that go outdoors and fight with other cats. Cat bites introduce bacteria deep under the skin, which quickly form a pus-filled pocket. Signs: painful swelling under the skin, especially on the face, neck, legs, or base of the tail, fever, lethargy, reduced appetite, and sometimes a small puncture hole with discharge. EMERGENCY — seek immediate care if: the wound is actively bleeding heavily, the cat is in obvious severe pain, has a fever over 104°F, or cannot walk normally. URGENT — see vet within 24 hours for any suspected abscess — they do not resolve on their own and require lancing, flushing, antibiotics, and sometimes a drain. Cats injured in fights are also at risk of FIV (feline AIDS) and FeLV transmission — blood testing is recommended after any fight wound. Never attempt to lance an abscess at home.

title: My dog is panting excessively — when is it a concern?
body: Panting is normal in dogs after exercise or in heat. It becomes a concern when: EMERGENCY — panting is combined with a distended abdomen and retching (bloat), the dog was in a hot environment and is disoriented, has bright red or pale gums, and cannot stand (heatstroke), or the dog is panting in a cool environment while at rest. URGENT — see vet within 24 hours if: panting is constant and unexplained, particularly at night or at rest, or is combined with coughing, exercise intolerance, or weight changes. Common medical causes of excessive panting: pain (dogs pant instead of crying), Cushing's disease (excess cortisol — also causes pot belly and hair loss), heart disease, respiratory disease, anemia, anxiety, and medication side effects (particularly steroids). If your dog's panting pattern has changed, it is worth a vet evaluation even without other obvious symptoms.

title: What is doxycycline used for in dogs and cats?
body: Doxycycline is a broad-spectrum antibiotic in the tetracycline class used in both dogs and cats to treat a wide range of bacterial and rickettsial infections. In veterinary medicine it is the antibiotic of choice for: Lyme disease, ehrlichiosis, anaplasmosis, and Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever (tick-borne diseases), as well as Mycoplasma infections, respiratory infections including kennel cough, and certain types of pneumonia. It is also used to treat Toxoplasma in cats and some atypical infections. It must always be given with food or a full syringe of water as it can cause severe esophageal irritation or ulcers if it sits in the esophagus — especially dangerous in cats. The typical course is 28–30 days for tick-borne diseases. Requires a prescription. Avoid in pregnant animals and young puppies/kittens under 6 months as it can affect developing teeth.

title: What is amoxicillin-clavulanate (Clavamox) used for?
body: Clavamox (amoxicillin-clavulanate) is a broad-spectrum antibiotic combination used in both dogs and cats to treat bacterial infections where bacteria may be resistant to amoxicillin alone. The clavulanate component inhibits beta-lactamase enzymes that some bacteria use to break down amoxicillin. Common uses include: skin and soft tissue infections, wound infections, urinary tract infections, dental infections, respiratory tract infections, and ear infections. It is available as tablets and oral liquid. It should be given with food to reduce stomach upset. Side effects may include vomiting, diarrhea, and loss of appetite. Do not use in pets with penicillin allergies. Complete the full course even if symptoms improve early — stopping antibiotics prematurely contributes to antibiotic resistance. Requires a prescription.

title: What is furosemide (Lasix) used for in pets?
body: Furosemide (Lasix) is a loop diuretic used in dogs and cats to remove excess fluid from the body. It works by preventing the kidneys from reabsorbing salt and water, increasing urine output. In veterinary medicine it is most commonly used to treat: congestive heart failure (removes fluid that accumulates around the lungs), pulmonary edema (fluid in the lungs), and other conditions causing fluid retention. It is a critical medication for dogs and cats with heart disease. Side effects include increased thirst and urination, electrolyte imbalances (particularly low potassium), dehydration, and kidney stress. Pets on furosemide require regular monitoring of kidney values and electrolytes. Do not restrict water access in pets on furosemide. Requires a prescription and ongoing veterinary monitoring.

title: What is phenobarbital used for in dogs?
body: Phenobarbital is the most commonly used anti-epileptic medication in dogs and one of the first-line treatments for canine epilepsy. It works by enhancing the activity of GABA, a calming neurotransmitter, reducing the frequency and severity of seizures. It is taken orally once or twice daily and must be given consistently — missed doses can trigger seizures. It takes 2 weeks to reach stable blood levels. Regular blood monitoring is essential — typically every 6 months — to check blood levels and liver function, as phenobarbital is metabolized by the liver and can cause liver enzyme elevation over time. Side effects are common initially and include: sedation, increased thirst and urination, increased appetite and weight gain. Most side effects diminish as the dog adjusts. Phenobarbital is a controlled substance. Never stop abruptly — withdrawal can trigger severe seizures.

title: What is cerenia (maropitant) used for in dogs and cats?
body: Cerenia (maropitant citrate) is an antiemetic medication used in dogs and cats to prevent and treat nausea and vomiting. It works by blocking substance P from binding to NK1 receptors in the brain, which is the primary pathway triggering vomiting. In dogs it is used for: motion sickness prevention (given 2 hours before travel), vomiting due to chemotherapy, post-operative nausea, parvovirus treatment protocols, and general vomiting from various causes. In cats it is used for similar indications. It is available as injectable and tablet form. Side effects are minimal — mild sedation and pain at injection site. It does not treat the underlying cause of vomiting but significantly improves comfort and reduces dehydration risk. Requires a prescription. For motion sickness, it should be given on an empty stomach 2 hours before travel for best effect.

title: What is enalapril used for in dogs with heart disease?
body: Enalapril is an ACE inhibitor (angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor) used in dogs to manage heart failure, particularly mitral valve disease (the most common heart disease in dogs) and dilated cardiomyopathy. It works by blocking the production of angiotensin II, a hormone that causes blood vessel constriction and fluid retention — reducing the heart's workload. It is often used alongside furosemide (diuretic) and pimobendan in the management of congestive heart failure. Benefits include: reduced blood pressure, decreased fluid retention, and slowing progression of heart enlargement. Side effects include: low blood pressure, increased kidney values, and cough (less common in dogs than humans). Regular blood pressure monitoring and kidney function testing are required. Requires a prescription and is taken daily long-term.

title: What is pimobendan (Vetmedin) and what does it do?
body: Pimobendan (Vetmedin) is a cardiac medication used in dogs with congestive heart failure due to mitral valve disease or dilated cardiomyopathy. It works through two mechanisms: it is both an inodilator (strengthens heart contractions) and a vasodilator (relaxes blood vessels). This combination improves cardiac output and reduces the heart's workload simultaneously. Vetmedin is given twice daily on an empty stomach — 1 hour before food for best absorption. It has been shown in clinical trials to extend survival time significantly in dogs with heart failure. It is also approved for use in the preclinical stage of mitral valve disease to delay onset of heart failure. Side effects are uncommon but may include decreased appetite, lethargy, and rarely arrhythmias. Requires a prescription and regular cardiac monitoring via echocardiogram and chest X-ray.

title: What is trazodone used for in dogs and cats?
body: Trazodone is a serotonin antagonist and reuptake inhibitor (SARI) used in dogs and increasingly in cats as a situational anxiolytic — a medication to reduce anxiety in specific stressful situations. Common uses include: pre-veterinary visit sedation, thunderstorm and fireworks anxiety, post-surgical confinement and recovery, and travel anxiety. It is given 1–2 hours before the anticipated stressful event. Unlike some sedatives, it reduces anxiety and promotes calm without causing significant sedation at typical doses. It can be combined with other anxiolytic medications. Side effects include mild sedation, incoordination, vomiting, and colitis. It should not be combined with MAOIs or other serotonergic medications without vet guidance due to risk of serotonin syndrome. Requires a prescription. It is an excellent option for the "just needs a vet visit" anxious patient.

title: What is the difference between Bravecto, NexGard, and Simparica?
body: Bravecto (fluralaner), NexGard (afoxolaner), and Simparica (sarolaner) are all oral isoxazoline class flea and tick preventives for dogs. They work by blocking chloride ion channels in the nervous system of insects and arachnids, causing paralysis and death. Key differences: Bravecto lasts 12 weeks per dose (chewable or topical spot-on), while NexGard and Simparica are monthly. Simparica Trio also includes heartworm prevention, roundworm, and hookworm coverage in one product. All three are highly effective against fleas and multiple tick species. The isoxazoline class carries an FDA warning about potential neurological adverse events (muscle tremors, ataxia, seizures) in rare cases, particularly in dogs with pre-existing neurological conditions. All require a prescription. In healthy dogs they are generally very well tolerated and are among the most effective parasite preventives available.

title: What is omeprazole (Prilosec) used for in dogs?
body: Omeprazole is a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) that reduces stomach acid production. In dogs it is used to treat and prevent: gastric and duodenal ulcers (particularly in dogs on long-term NSAIDs which can damage the stomach lining), acid reflux (gastroesophageal reflux), esophagitis (inflammation of the esophagus), Helicobacter pylori infection, and stomach protection during illness or stress. It is given once daily, ideally in the morning before the first meal. It takes 3–5 days to reach full effect. Human omeprazole (Prilosec OTC) is commonly used in dogs as it is identical to the veterinary formulation — however always confirm the dose with your vet before administering any human medication. Side effects are uncommon. Long-term use may affect vitamin B12 absorption and alter gut microbiome. Requires veterinary guidance for dosing.

title: ¿Qué vacunas necesita mi cachorro?
body: Las vacunas esenciales para cachorros incluyen: DA2PP o DHPP (Moquillo, Parvovirus, Adenovirus, Parainfluenza) que comienza a las 6–8 semanas y se repite cada 3–4 semanas hasta las 16 semanas. La vacuna contra la rabia se aplica a las 12–16 semanas. Las vacunas no esenciales como Bordetella (tos de las perreras), Leptospirosis e Influenza Canina se recomiendan según el estilo de vida y el riesgo. Su veterinario creará un calendario adaptado a su cachorro. Es fundamental completar toda la serie de vacunas — un cachorro no está completamente protegido hasta 1–2 semanas después de la última dosis.

title: ¿Con qué frecuencia debo desparasitar a mi perro?
body: Los cachorros deben desparasitarse a las 2, 4, 6 y 8 semanas de edad, y luego mensualmente hasta los 6 meses. Los perros adultos deben desparasitarse al menos 2–4 veces al año según su estilo de vida. Los perros que cazan, comen animales silvestres o pasan tiempo al aire libre necesitan tratamientos más frecuentes. La prevención mensual contra el gusano del corazón también cubre algunos parásitos intestinales. Solicite a su veterinario un examen fecal anual para detectar parásitos que no estén cubiertos por los desparasitantes estándar.

title: ¿Por qué mi gato tira las cosas al suelo?
body: Los gatos tiran objetos de las superficies por varias razones documentadas. Instinto de caza: golpear objetos imita el comportamiento de caza. Búsqueda de atención: si tirar cosas genera una reacción, el gato aprende que es una forma efectiva de llamar la atención. Curiosidad y exploración: los gatos usan sus patas para explorar las propiedades físicas de los objetos. Aburrimiento o falta de estimulación. El comportamiento es normal y no indica problemas de conducta. Para reducirlo: proporcione suficiente juego y enriquecimiento, ignore el comportamiento para no reforzarlo con atención, y asegure los objetos valiosos.

title: ¿Qué es el parvovirus canino y qué tan peligroso es?
body: El parvovirus canino (CPV) es una enfermedad viral altamente contagiosa y potencialmente mortal que afecta principalmente a los cachorros y perros no vacunados. Ataca el revestimiento del intestino delgado causando diarrea con sangre grave, vómitos, letargo y pérdida del apetito. El virus se transmite a través del contacto directo con heces infectadas y superficies contaminadas — puede sobrevivir en superficies durante meses o años. El tratamiento es de apoyo (fluidos IV, medicación antiemética, antibióticos) y las tasas de supervivencia con tratamiento agresivo son del 68–92%. Sin tratamiento la supervivencia cae a menos del 10%. La prevención es mediante vacunación — la vacuna contra el parvovirus es una vacuna esencial para todos los perros.

title: Mi gato tiene dificultad para respirar — ¿es una emergencia?
body: Sí — la respiración con la boca abierta en un gato siempre es una emergencia. A diferencia de los perros, los gatos no jadean normalmente. La respiración con la boca abierta en gatos indica dificultad respiratoria grave y requiere atención veterinaria de emergencia inmediata. No espere. Al transportar al gato, mantenga el ambiente tranquilo, minimice la manipulación y asegure una buena circulación de aire. Causas comunes: insuficiencia cardíaca congestiva, derrame pleural (líquido en la cavidad torácica), asma felina, neumonía y trauma torácico. La respiración rápida y superficial, el cuello extendido hacia adelante y los codos hacia afuera, las encías azules o grises son señales de privación grave de oxígeno. Vaya siempre al veterinario de emergencias.

title: Mi perro tiene las orejas rojas y con picor — ¿qué podría ser?
body: Los problemas de oído están entre las razones más comunes por las que los perros visitan al veterinario. Signos de infección de oído: sacudidas de cabeza, rascado en las orejas, secreción oscura, olor en las orejas, enrojecimiento o hinchazón del canal auditivo, inclinación de la cabeza y dolor al tocar las orejas. URGENTE — consulte al veterinario en 48 horas ante cualquier infección de oído sospechada — empeoran rápidamente. Causas comunes: sobrecrecimiento de levaduras (más común — secreción marrón, olor a moho), infección bacteriana (secreción amarillo-verdosa, olor fétido), ácaros del oído (residuos oscuros y quebradizos), cuerpo extraño o alergias. Nunca inserte hisopos en el canal auditivo de un perro.

title: ¿Para qué se usa la metronidazola en perros y gatos?
body: La metronidazol (Flagyl) es un antibiótico y antiprotozoario utilizado tanto en perros como en gatos para tratar diversas afecciones gastrointestinales. Se prescribe comúnmente para diarrea causada por sobrecrecimiento bacteriano, infección por Giardia, infección por Clostridium y enfermedad inflamatoria intestinal. Requiere receta. Los efectos secundarios comunes incluyen babeo, náuseas y vómitos — dar con comida generalmente ayuda. Los efectos secundarios neurológicos (tambaleo, desorientación) son raros pero graves y requieren suspender el medicamento de inmediato. No debe usarse en animales embarazadas. El curso típico es de 5–10 días.

title: ¿Para qué se usa el carprofen (Rimadyl) en perros?
body: El carprofen (Rimadyl) es un antiinflamatorio no esteroideo (AINE) utilizado en perros para aliviar el dolor y la inflamación. Se prescribe comúnmente para la osteoartritis, el dolor posquirúrgico y el dolor de tejidos blandos. Siempre debe administrarse con comida para reducir el malestar estomacal. Requiere receta y monitoreo regular — el uso a largo plazo requiere análisis de sangre periódicos para verificar la función hepática y renal. Nunca debe combinarse con otros AINEs o esteroides. Nunca le dé a su perro AINEs humanos (ibuprofeno, aspirina, naproxeno) — son tóxicos para los perros. Señales de reacción adversa: vómitos, diarrea, heces negras alquitranadas — suspenda el medicamento y contacte a su veterinario de inmediato.

title: Señales de emergencia que siempre requieren atención veterinaria inmediata
body: Los siguientes síntomas en cualquier mascota requieren atención veterinaria de emergencia inmediata: convulsiones o ataques, colapso o incapacidad repentina para ponerse de pie, dificultad grave para respirar o respiración con la boca abierta en gatos, ingestión sospechosa de veneno o toxina, sangrado incontrolable, abdomen distendido especialmente con arcadas improductivas en perros, lesión en el ojo o pérdida repentina de visión, incapacidad para orinar especialmente en gatos machos, encías pálidas, blancas, azules o grises, trauma por vehículo, caída de altura o ataque de otro animal. Ante la duda, llame siempre — una llamada de 2 minutos al veterinario de emergencias puede salvar la vida de su mascota.

title: How often do rabbits need veterinary care?
body: Rabbits require at least an annual wellness exam. They are prey animals and hide illness expertly — by the time signs are obvious, a rabbit may be critically ill. Key health concerns in rabbits: dental disease (teeth grow continuously and can cause painful malocclusion), GI stasis (the most common life-threatening emergency — digestive system slows or stops), ear mites, respiratory infections, and uterine cancer in unspayed females (affects up to 80% of intact females over 5 years). Rabbits should be spayed (females) or neutered (males) for health and behavioral reasons. Vaccines are not required in the US but are recommended in some countries for rabbit hemorrhagic disease. A rabbit that stops eating for more than 12 hours requires same-day veterinary attention.

title: What are signs of illness in birds?
body: Birds hide illness until they are critically ill — this is a survival instinct from being prey animals. Any of the following signs require a vet visit within 24 hours or sooner: sitting at the bottom of the cage (serious sign — healthy birds perch), fluffed feathers for extended periods, eyes partially or fully closed during the day, discharge from eyes or nares (nostrils), changes in droppings (color, consistency, volume — learn your bird's normal baseline), reduced vocalization, tail bobbing with each breath (indicates respiratory distress), loss of grip strength, weight loss (weigh weekly with a kitchen scale), regurgitation beyond normal mate-feeding behavior, or bleeding from any site. A lethargic bird that has not eaten or drunk for 12 hours is an emergency. Find an avian-certified veterinarian — general practice vets may not be equipped for avian medicine.

title: How do I know if my rabbit has GI stasis?
body: Gastrointestinal stasis (GI stasis) is the most common veterinary emergency in rabbits and can be fatal within 24–48 hours if untreated. The GI tract slows or stops completely, leading to gas buildup, toxin accumulation, and organ failure. Signs: no droppings or very small, dry, misshapen droppings, complete refusal to eat — even favorite foods, hunched posture with teeth grinding (pain), distended or hard abdomen, complete inactivity, and pressing belly to the ground. Any rabbit that has not produced droppings in 12 hours or has not eaten in 12 hours must be seen by a vet the same day. Do not wait overnight. Treatment includes: gut motility drugs, pain relief, IV or subcutaneous fluids, syringe feeding, and treating the underlying cause (stress, dental pain, dehydration, or low fiber diet). Prevention: unlimited timothy hay is the most important factor in rabbit GI health.

title: What should I feed my rabbit?
body: The foundation of a healthy rabbit diet is unlimited grass hay — timothy hay for adults, alfalfa only for rabbits under 6 months. Hay should make up 80% of the diet and must be available at all times. It is essential for dental health (wears down continuously growing teeth), GI motility, and preventing obesity. Fresh leafy greens (romaine lettuce, cilantro, parsley, basil, kale in moderation) should be offered daily — about 1 cup per 2 lbs of body weight. Pellets should be limited — maximum 1/4 cup per 5 lbs per day of plain timothy-based pellets. Avoid: iceberg lettuce (no nutrition), sugary fruits (only as rare treats), crackers, bread, seeds, and any human food. Fresh water must always be available. Rabbits that do not eat enough hay are at high risk for dental disease and GI stasis.
